subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given,

We have to form 6 digit number using the digits 4, 5 & 9 and for number should be divisible by 6 which means that the addition of number should be divisible by 3 and unit place should be even, so we will make following cases:

Case 1 The number is 444444 which can be arranged in 1 way,

Case 2 when the number is formed using the digits 4,4,4,5,9 and last place is fixed with 4, that can be arranged in 5!3!=20 ways

Case 3 when number is formed using the digits 4,4,5,5,5 and last place is fixed with 4, that can be arranged in 5!3!2!=10 ways

Case 4 when number is formed using the digits 4,4,9,9,9 and last place is fixed by 4, that can be arranged in 5!3!2!=10 ways

Case 5 when number is formed using the digits 4,5,5,9,9 and last place is fixed with 4, that can be arranged in 5!2!2!=30 ways

Case 6 when number is formed using the digits 5,9,9,9,9 and last place is fixed with 4, that can be arranged in 5!4!=5 ways

Case 7 when number is formed using the digits 5,5,5,5,9 and last place is fixed with 4, that can be arranged in 5!4!=5 ways

Now adding all the cases we get, 1+20+10+10+30+5+5=81 ways.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
We know that for making a triangle, we require three points, which are not on a line.

Here, we have total 3+5+6=14 points out of which 3, 5, 6 points respectively, on AB, BC and CA are collinear.

Thus, the total Number of triangles formed

=C314-C33-C35-C36

=14!3!·11!-3!3!·0!-5!3!·2!-6!3!·3!

=14×13×12×11!3×2×1×11!-1-5×4×3!3!×2×1-6×5×4×3!3×2×1×3!

364-1-10-20=333.

Hence, total number of triangles is 333.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
To ensure no bag is empty, exactly one bag must contain 2 books and the other two bags must contain 1 book each.

The number of ways to choose 2 books out of 4 to be kept together is $^{4}C_{2} = 6$

Now, we have 3 units (one pair of books and two single books) to be distributed into the 3 different bags.

The number of ways to distribute these 3 units into 3 bags is $3! = 6$

Total number of ways = $6 \times 6 = 36$

Answer: $36$

$\begin{aligned} & 7 \mathrm{n}=4+11 \mathrm{r} \\ & \frac{{ }^n C_r}{{ }^{n-1} C_{r-1}}=\frac{35}{21} \\ & \frac{n !}{r !(n-r) !}=\frac{(r-1) !(n-r) !}{(n-1) !}=\frac{5}{3} \\ & \frac{n}{r}=\frac{5}{3} \\ & 3 n=5 r \\ & \text { By solving } r=6 \quad n=10 \\ & 2 n+5 r=50\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Distributing 16 identical oranges to 4 children with each getting at least 1.
Let $x_i \ge 1$ for $i = 1,2,3,4$ and $x_1+x_2+x_3+x_4 = 16$.
Substituting $y_i = x_i - 1 \ge 0$: $y_1+y_2+y_3+y_4 = 12$.
By stars and bars, number of ways $= \binom{12+3}{3} = \binom{15}{3} = \frac{15 \times 14 \times 13}{6} = 455$.

No of such arrangements =4!.3!

VOWELS all the consonants never come together =Total - all consonants come together =6!-4!×3!=576

subject: Mathematics
solution:
In 8 digits numbers, 4 places are odd places.
Also, in the given 8 digits, there are three odd digits 1,1 and 3.
No. of ways three odd digits arranged at
$$
\text { four even places }=\frac{4 P_3}{2 !}=\frac{4 !}{2 !}
$$
No. of ways the remaining five digits 2 , 2, 2, 4 and 4 arranged at remaining five places $=\frac{5 !}{3 ! 2 !}$
Hence, required number of 8 digits number $=\frac{4 !}{2 !} \times \frac{5 !}{3 ! 2 !}=120$

solution:
We have 4 identical shelves in which any number of shelves can be empty.

So, we will put 3, 2, 1, 0 shelves empty one by one.

Also, the non-empty shelves won't require internal arrangement as all the shelves are identical.  

3 Shelf empty: 8,0,0,0→1 way

2 shelf empty: (7,1,0,0)(6,2,0,0)(5,3,0,0)(4,4,0,0)→4 ways

1 shelf empty: (6,1,1,0)(3,3,2,0)(5,2,1,0)(4,2,2,0)(4,3,1,0)→5ways

0 shelf empty: (1,2,3,2)(5,1,1,1)(2,2,2,2)(3,3,1,1)(4,2,1,1)→5 ways

Total =15 ways

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The number of triangles formed by joining the vertices of an $n$-sided regular polygon is given by $p_n = ^{n}C_{3}$.

Given that $p_{n+1} - p_n = 66$.

$^{n+1}C_{3} - ^{n}C_{3} = 66$

Using the property $^{n+1}C_{r} - ^{n}C_{r} = ^{n}C_{r-1}$, we get:

$^{n}C_{2} = 66$

$\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2} = 66$

$n(n-1) = 132$

$n^2 - n - 132 = 0$

$(n-12)(n+11) = 0$

Since $n$ must be a positive integer, $n = 12$.

The prime factorization of $12$ is $2^2 \times 3$. The distinct prime divisors of $12$ are $2$ and $3$.

The sum of all distinct prime divisors of $n$ is $2 + 3 = 5$.

Answer: $5$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given,

Numbers 1,3,5,7,9

Now to form number between 5000-10000

_⏟5,7,9_⏟4 ways_⏟3 ways_⏟2 ways

Here at 1000's place should be taken by 5, 7 & 9

So for 1000's place there will be 3 ways,

Now for hundredth place there will be 4 ways,

For ten's place there will be 3 ways,

And for unit place there will be 2 ways,

So total ways will be 3×4×3×2=72 ways.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
To find total numbers between 1000 and 3000 divisible by 4 using the digits 1,2,3,4,5,6,

We will solve in two cases.

Case I : When first digit is 1.

Then last two digits can be 24,32,36,52,56 or 64

So, total ways of choosing last two digit is 6 and second digit will be chosen in 3 ways

So, number of such numbers =6×3=18

Case II: When first digit is 2

Then last two digits can be 16,36,56 or 64

So, total ways of choosing last two digit is 4 and second digit will be chosen in 3 

So, number of such numbers =4×3=12

Total numbers of numbers =18+12=30

subject: Mathematics
solution:
A number is divisible by 6 when it is divisible by 2 and 3 both.

The three digit number can be a¯b¯2¯.

a+b can be 4,7,10

If a+b=4 then (a, b) can be (1,3), (3,1), (2,2)

If a+b=7 then (a, b) can be(2,5),(5,2),(3,4),(4,3) 

If a+b=10 then (a, b) can be (5,5)

So, 8 such cases when 2 is at unit's place

Similarly, there exist 8 such cases when 4 is at unit's place.

Total =16 cases.

Hence this is the required answer.

solution:
Let the number of men participating in tournament =m.

Number of women participating in tournament =2.

Each player will play 2 games with every other player and two women will play 2 games with each man.

Total numbers of games played by women =2×2m=4m

Total number of games played by men =2·mC2

From given data

2· mC2-4m=66

⇒2mm-12-4m=66

⇒m2-5m-66=0

⇒m-11m+6=0

⇒m=11 or -6 but m∈N, m≠-6

⇒m∈10,12.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let $\mathrm{m}=6 \mathrm{a}, \mathrm{n}=6 \mathrm{~b}$
$\mathrm{m} \lt \mathrm{n} \Rightarrow \mathrm{a} \lt \mathrm{b}$
where $\mathrm{a} \& \mathrm{~b}$ are co-prime numbers also since $m \& n$ are 2 digit nos, so
$10 \leq \mathrm{m} \leq 99 \& 10 \leq \mathrm{n} \leq 99$
i.e. $2 \leq \mathrm{a} \leq 16 \& 2 \leq \mathrm{b} \leq 16$
( $\because$ a is integer)
Now
$2 \leq \mathrm{a} \lt \mathrm{b} \leq 16 \& \quad \mathrm{a} \& \mathrm{~b}$ are co-prime
$\therefore$ if
$\mathrm{a}=2, \mathrm{~b}=3,5,7,9,11,13,15$
$a=3, b=4,5,7,8,10,11,13,14,16$
$a=4, b=5,7,9,11,13,15$

$\mathrm{a}=12, \mathrm{~b}=13$
$a=13, b=14,15,16$
$\mathrm{a}=14, \mathrm{~b}=15$
$\mathrm{a}=15, \mathrm{~b}=16$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The given word is INCONSEQUENTIAL.

The vowels present in the word are I, O, E, U, A. There are $5$ distinct vowels.

The consonants present in the word are N, C, S, Q, T, L. There are $6$ distinct consonants.

We need to form $4$-letter words consisting of $2$ vowels and $2$ consonants without repeating any letter. This means all $4$ letters in the formed word must be distinct.

Number of ways to select $2$ distinct vowels from $5$ distinct vowels = $^{5}C_{2} = \dfrac{5 \times 4}{2} = 10$

Number of ways to select $2$ distinct consonants from $6$ distinct consonants = $^{6}C_{2} = \dfrac{6 \times 5}{2} = 15$

Number of ways to arrange the $4$ selected letters = $4! = 24$

Total number of $4$-letter words that can be formed = $10 \times 15 \times 24 = 3600$

Answer: $3600$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
A seven-digit number is to be formed using the $5$ digits $1, 2, 3, 5,$ and $7$. Since each digit must be used at least once, the remaining $2$ places can be filled by either repeating one digit two more times, or repeating two digits one more time each.

This gives rise to two cases for the frequencies of the digits:

Case 1: One digit appears $3$ times and the other four digits appear $1$ time each.
The number of ways to choose the digit that appears $3$ times is $^{5}C_{1}$.
The number of arrangements of these $7$ digits is $\dfrac{7!}{3!}$.
Number of numbers formed in this case = $^{5}C_{1} \times \dfrac{7!}{3!} = 5 \times \dfrac{5040}{6} = 4200$.

Case 2: Two digits appear $2$ times each and the other three digits appear $1$ time each.
The number of ways to choose the two digits that appear $2$ times is $^{5}C_{2}$.
The number of arrangements of these $7$ digits is $\dfrac{7!}{2!2!}$.
Number of numbers formed in this case = $^{5}C_{2} \times \dfrac{7!}{2!2!} = 10 \times \dfrac{5040}{4} = 12600$.

Total number of seven-digit numbers = $4200 + 12600 = 16800$.

Answer: $16800$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given,

7 boys and 5 girls are to be seated around a circular such that no two girls to be seated together,

Now we know that n objects can be arranged in a circle in (n-1)! ways.

Let us first arrange 7 boys in circular arrangement in 7-1! ways.

Now there will be 7 gaps.

So let us select any 5 gaps out of 7 gaps and arrange 5 girls in the chosen gaps. This can be done in C57×5! ways.

Hence, required arrangements are 6!×C57×5!

=6×5!×7×62×5!

=1265!2.

Therefore, required arrangements are 1265!2

So, the remaining 15 apples are to be distributed such that each children can get any number of apples.

So, the required number of ways will be given by N=Cr-1n+r-1.

⇒N=C215+3-1

⇒N=C217

⇒N=17×162

⇒N=136

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The total possible 4 digit numbers are 9×10×10×10=9000

Let, X is the 4 digit number divisible by 3

1002, 1005, ………, 9999

a=1002, d=3 & an=9999

9999=1002+n3-13

n3=3000

Let, Y is the 4 digit numbers divisible by 7

1001,1008, ………, 9996

a=1001, d=7 & an=9996

9996=1001+n7-17

n7=1286

Now, 4 digit numbers divisible by both 3 and 7

1008,1029,...........9996

a=1008, d=21, an=9996

Now, 9996=1008+n-121

n=429

Hence,  4 digit numbers neither divisible by 7 nor 3

=9000-1286-3000+429

=5143
